entries.title: *Mutus Liber* — title plate ("Mutus Liber, in quo tamen...")
entries.creator: 'Altus' (attrib. Isaac Baulot)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per the source copy (Internet Archive scan)]
entries.summary:
The opening plate of the *Mutus Liber* — the celebrated "wordless book" of alchemy, printed at La Rochelle in 1677 under the pseudonym 'Altus' (generally identified with Isaac Baulot). This sheet carries the work's only substantial text: its Latin title-device.

## Iconography
The famous first emblem: a man sleeps at the foot of a great ladder set against a rocky landscape while two trumpet-bearing angels ascend and descend its rungs — a clear borrowing from Jacob's dream at Bethel (Genesis 28). A banderole bears the punning Latin legend that the book is mute "and yet [*in quo tamen*] all of Hermetic philosophy is set forth in hieroglyphic figures." Roses and emblematic devices flank the scene.

## Significance
The sleeping dreamer and the ladder announce the book's whole method: the Work is to be received as a revelation, read in pictures rather than spelled out. The [[motif:jacob's ladder|ladder]] figures the graded ascent of matter and soul through the stages of the *opus*; the angels are the celestial influences (the dew) that descend to the laboratory. Frequently reprinted in Manget's *Bibliotheca Chemica Curiosa* (1702), the *Mutus Liber* became a touchstone of the idea that alchemy is an essentially visionary, visual science.

## For artists & game designers
A ready-made "vision" frame: a sleeper, a celestial ladder, ascending and descending messengers — drop-in iconography for a dream-sequence or a quest-giving revelation.

citations.text: Adam McLean, *A Commentary on the Mutus Liber* (Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).

entries.title: *Mutus Liber* — the dew-gathering on the field
entries.creator: 'Altus' (attrib. Isaac Baulot)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per the source copy (Internet Archive scan)]
entries.summary:
A working plate from the *Mutus Liber*, the wordless 1677 picture-book of the alchemical Work, showing the celebrated dew-harvest by the praying couple.

## Iconography
Under a sky governed by a personified Sun and Moon, a man and a woman spread linen cloths over a meadow and wring the gathered moisture into vessels. Sheep graze nearby; the labour proceeds in the open air. The two operators kneel and pray at the margins of the scene, a recurring device throughout the book.

## Significance
The sheets soaked at dawn collect the *ros caeli*, the heavenly dew — for Altus the prime philosophical matter, condensed from the influences of [[motif:sol and luna|Sol and Luna]]. The shared labour of husband and wife embodies the alchemical coniunctio of male and female principles, while their prayer insists that the Work is a *donum Dei*, granted only to the pious. The dew-collection is the single most reproduced image of the *Mutus Liber* and a favourite of later esoteric writers who read it as both chemistry and devotion.

## For artists & game designers
A gentle, eerie gathering ritual: figures wringing starlit dew from cloths at dawn — perfect for a reagent-collection mechanic that feels sacred rather than industrial.

citations.text: Adam McLean, *A Commentary on the Mutus Liber* (Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).

entries.title: *Mutus Liber* — furnace and vessel operations
entries.creator: 'Altus' (attrib. Isaac Baulot)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per the source copy (Library of Congress / Internet Archive scan)]
entries.summary:
A laboratory plate from the *Mutus Liber* (1677), the wordless emblem-book that sets out the entire Hermetic Work in pictures alone.

## Iconography
The operators tend the indoor phase of the *opus*: a furnace or athanor heats a flask, and sealed glass vessels containing the maturing matter are arranged across registers of the plate. The man and woman repeat their kneeling, hands-clasped postures; small numbered or lettered devices mark the sequence of steps. Solar and lunar emblems hover over the operations to keep the celestial-laboratory correspondence in view.

## Significance
Where the outdoor plates harvest the dew, this sheet carries the matter indoors for *digestion*, *distillation* and *circulation* in the heat of the [[motif:athanor|athanor]]. The flasks register the colour-changes of the Work — the *nigredo*, *albedo* and *rubedo* by which alchemists read the progress of the stone. The persistence of the praying couple binds furnace-craft to devotion: the fire is tended, but the outcome is prayed for.

## For artists & game designers
A clean visual grammar for a crafting-station screen: numbered vessels, a glowing furnace, two attendants — an emblem of process you can map directly onto a brewing or transmutation UI.

citations.text: Adam McLean, *A Commentary on the Mutus Liber* (Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).

entries.title: *Mutus Liber* — two putti holding the philosophers' stone
entries.creator: 'Altus' (attrib. Isaac Baulot)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per the source copy (Internet Archive scan)]
entries.summary:
An emblematic plate from the *Mutus Liber* (1677) in which two winged putti present the fruit of the Work — the philosophers' stone shown as a globe or medallion enclosing a further image.

## Iconography
Two small winged genii flank and uphold a central round object — a sphere or jewel that itself contains a scene, an emblem nested within the emblem. The composition is heraldic and presentational: the putti act as supporters, displaying the perfected stone as one would display a crest or a relic.

## Significance
The nested image is a visual pun on the stone's nature: the *lapis* is said to contain the whole cosmos in little, a microcosm. By giving it to childlike, airy spirits to hold aloft, Altus frames the completed Work as a gift received rather than seized — innocence and grace presenting the [[motif:philosophers' stone|philosophers' stone]]. Putti as bearers of arcane fruit are a stock device of Baroque emblematics that the *Mutus Liber* turns to alchemical ends.

## For artists & game designers
A trophy-reveal composition: cherubic supporters lifting a glowing relic that holds a tiny world inside it — ideal art for an end-of-quest reward or an artifact-inspection screen.

citations.text: Adam McLean, *A Commentary on the Mutus Liber* (Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).

entries.title: *Mutus Liber* — cosmological diagram of concentric circles
entries.creator: 'Altus' (attrib. Isaac Baulot)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per the source copy (Library of Congress / Internet Archive scan)]
entries.summary:
A cosmological plate associated with the *Mutus Liber* corpus, rendering creation as a nest of concentric circles — the macrocosm against which the alchemical labour is set.

## Iconography
Rings within rings describe the ordered cosmos: an outermost heaven encloses the planetary spheres, the elements and a central earth or sun, in the manner of a creation-diagram. Rays, emblematic devices or labels mark the descent of influence from the circumference toward the centre. The image is essentially diagrammatic rather than narrative.

## Significance
The ringed cosmos states the doctrine on which the whole *Mutus Liber* depends: *as above, so below*. The dew gathered in the field plates and the matter cooked in the flasks are condensations of the same celestial powers mapped here. Concentric creation-schemes of this kind descend from Fludd and the wider Hermetic tradition; placed beside the laboratory scenes they assert that the operator's furnace is a small replica of the heavens.

## For artists & game designers
A self-contained cosmic dial: nested spheres radiating influence inward — usable as a map screen, a magic-system diagram, or a loading-screen mandala.

citations.text: Robert Fludd, *Utriusque Cosmi ... Historia* (1617–21), for the concentric-cosmos tradition.

entries.title: *Mutus Liber* — cover / title page of a printed edition
entries.creator: 'Altus' (attrib. Isaac Baulot)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per the source copy (Internet Archive scan)]
entries.summary:
The cover or title page of a printed edition of the *Mutus Liber* — a bibliographic, largely textual leaf rather than one of the book's emblematic plates.

## Iconography
A letterpress title page bearing the work's name and imprint, with little or no figurative imagery. It serves to identify the edition and frame the silent plates that follow.

## Significance
Though not itself an emblem, the title leaf is part of the book's deliberate paradox: a "silent book" still required a spoken title to enter the bibliographic world, and the wording ("*Mutus Liber, in quo tamen tota Philosophia Hermetica ... figuris hieroglyphicis depingitur*") advertises the wordless method on the one page that uses words. Included here for completeness of the edition rather than for its iconography.

## For artists & game designers
Useful as a reference frontispiece or as set-dressing — a period title page to establish a found-grimoire before the player turns to the picture plates.

entries.title: *Mutus Liber* — plate from the 1702 *Bibliotheca Chemica* reprint
entries.creator: 'Altus' (attrib. Isaac Baulot); engraved for J.-J. Manget
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per the source copy (Internet Archive scan)]
entries.summary:
A plate of the *Mutus Liber* as re-engraved for Jean-Jacques Manget's *Bibliotheca Chemica Curiosa* (Geneva, 1702), the printing through which the silent book reached its widest early audience.

## Iconography
The scene reproduces the book's signature programme: the labouring couple at the celestial work — gathering dew, tending vessels and praying — beneath emblems of Sun and Moon. The re-engraving is slightly recut from the 1677 La Rochelle plates but keeps the figures and their devout postures.

## Significance
Manget's monumental anthology gave the *Mutus Liber* canonical status and is the version cited by most later esotericists. The continuity of the imagery across editions — outdoor harvest, indoor furnace, ascent of the soul — let the [[motif:dew collection|dew-gathering]] become shorthand for the whole Hermetic Work in eighteenth- and nineteenth-century occult literature.

## For artists & game designers
A second 'take' on the dew-harvest, handy when you need a slightly different period engraving of the same ritual for variety across a set of cards or pages.

citations.text: 'Altus' (attrib. Isaac Baulot), *Mutus Liber*, in J.-J. Manget, *Bibliotheca Chemica Curiosa* (Geneva, 1702).
Adam McLean, *A Commentary on the Mutus Liber* (Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).

entries.title: *Mutus Liber* — plate 12 (the harvest and the reaper)
entries.creator: 'Altus' (attrib. Isaac Baulot)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per the source copy (Internet Archive scan)]
entries.summary:
Plate 12 of the *Mutus Liber* (1677), a late emblem in the sequence where the long labour begins to bear its harvest.

## Iconography
The operators are shown reaping or gathering the matured fruit of the Work — a harvest scene with sheaves, sickle or scythe — and again kneeling in prayer at the picture's edges. The composition turns the earlier sowing-and-watering imagery (the dew plates) toward its conclusion: what was gathered as dew and cooked in the flasks is now cut and brought in.

## Significance
The agricultural metaphor governs the whole book — the alchemist as husbandman who plants, waters and finally reaps. The harvest plate marks *fixation* and the gathering of the perfected stone, the *messis* (harvest) that crowns the patient cultivation of the matter. The recurring prayer keeps the outcome framed as grace: the reaper gathers only what heaven has ripened.

## For artists & game designers
A satisfying "payoff" panel for a long crafting arc: the same two figures who gathered dew now reap a glowing harvest — a natural closing image for a multi-stage quest.

citations.text: Adam McLean, *A Commentary on the Mutus Liber* (Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
